allow: Bash("think of these data types as the **building blocks** of python. like actual bricks, you can't break them down into anything more useful — try to split an integer and you'll just get... well, nothing.\n",)
Bash("there are four atomic data types in python:\n",)

Bash("> **quick note on the environment:** this notebook runs on jupyter, which lets you execute code cell-by-cell, see results inline, and experiment freely. it's like having a conversation with python — you say something, it responds, and you build on that.\n",)
Bash("> **pro tip:** don't just read — run the cells, tweak the values, and see what happens. that's where the real learning kicks in.\n",)
Bash("computers love numbers. they're straightforward to store and lightning-fast to compute with. we'll break this down into two types:\n",)
Bash("you can do all the usual math: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and even exponentiation. let's see it in action.\n",)
Bash("an integer is a whole number — no decimals, no fractions. it's what you'd use for counting things, like \"the 3rd item in a list\" or \"page 42.\"\n",)
Bash("### quick note on variable assignment\n",)
Bash("before we go further, let's talk about how to store values:\n",)
Bash("| `==` | **comparison** — checks if two values are equal | `x == 5` returns `true` |\n",)
Bash("> ⚠️ common mistake: `=` assigns, `==` compares. don't mix them up!")
Bash("this is your window into what python is thinking. it displays output on the console, and you can pass multiple values at once:")
Bash("now that we've got numbers in memory, let's do some math. here's what python offers:\n",)
Bash("> just like in math class, **bedmas** applies (brackets, exponents, division/multiplication, addition/subtraction).")
Bash("the modulo (`%`) operator gives you the **remainder** after division. it's surprisingly useful!\n",)

Bash("**real-world example:** say you're cycling through a deck of 52 cards and have a large random number. taking `number % 52` always gives you a result between 0 and 51 — perfect for picking a card.\n",)
Bash("notice something about `a / b` above? it has a decimal point. that means it's no longer an `int` — it's a......\n",)
Bash("here's a rule worth memorizing: **division always returns a `float`**, even if the result is a whole number.\n",)
Bash("sometimes you need to convert between types — say, turning a `float` into an `int`, or a number into a string. this is called **coercion** or **casting**.\n",)
Bash("> ⚠️ **important:** `int()` **truncates** (chops off decimals), it does **not** round!\n",)
Bash("booleans are python's way of representing **truth**: `true` or `false`. under the hood, `true` is `1` and `false` is `0`, but you'll rarely need to think about that.\n",)
Bash("you get booleans whenever you compare values:\n",)
Bash("> 💡 **note:** when we reassign `a` and `b` with new values, the old values are gone. python's garbage collector cleans them up automatically.")
Bash("- **`==`** checks if two values are **equal**\n",)
Bash("- **`is`** checks if two variables point to the **same object in memory**\n",)
Bash("for simple values like numbers, they often behave the same. but for complex objects (lists, dicts), the distinction matters. let's see it:")
Bash("booleans are the backbone of **decision-making** in your code. they let your program take different paths based on conditions.\n",)
Bash("here's a classic example — checking if a number is odd or even:\n",)
Bash("> we'll cover flow control in depth in session 3, but here's a sneak peek:\n",)
Bash("> - if `true`, it runs the indented code block\n",)
Bash("> - if `false`, it skips to `else` (if present)\n",)
Bash("> - you can nest `if-else` blocks for more complex logic")
Bash("text in programming is called a **string** (`str` for short). strings are how you handle anything textual — names, messages, file paths, you name it.\n",)
Bash("> - if your string contains single quotes, wrap it in double quotes (and vice versa)\n",)
Bash("### ⚠️ common pitfall: adding strings and numbers\n",)
Bash("one of the most common beginner errors is trying to add a string and a number together. python won't guess what you mean — you need to be explicit.")
Bash("most of the time, you'll want to inject variables into strings. the modern python way is using **f-strings** (formatted string literals):\n",)
Bash("greeting = f\"hello, {name}!\"  # the 'f' prefix enables formatting\n",)
Bash("you can even format numbers right in the string — like limiting decimal places:\n",)
Bash("see this [cookbook](https://mkaz.blog/code/python-string-format-cookbook/) for more formatting options!")
Bash("need a block of text? use triple double-quotes `\"\"\"`:")
Bash("alternatively, use the newline character `\\n`. but honestly, triple quotes are much more readable!")
Bash("adding strings together is called **concatenation**. simple, but watch out — python won't add spaces for you!")
Bash("for joining multiple strings with a separator, `.join()` is cleaner:")
Bash("you can access individual characters (or ranges of characters) in a string using **square brackets** `[]`.\n",)
Bash("python uses **zero-based indexing** — the first character is at position `0`, not `1`. so to get the '`d`' in \"richard\", you'd use `[6]`.\n",)
Bash("you can also use **negative indices** to count from the end: `[-1]` is the last character, `[-2]` is second-to-last, etc.\n",)
Bash("for ranges (slicing), use `[start:stop]`. the `stop` position is **exclusive** — it goes up to, but not including, that index.\n",)
Bash("> **think of it this way:** indices mark the *gaps between* characters, not the characters themselves. so `[0:2]` captures everything between gap 0 and gap 2.")
Bash("you can also add a **step** to skip characters: `[start:stop:step]`.\n",)
Bash("if you omit `start` or `stop`, python assumes the beginning or end respectively. so `[3:]` means \"everything from position 3 onwards.\"")
Bash("now, what happens with a step of `-1`? 🤔")
Bash("strings come with built-in methods for common operations. a **method** is just a function that belongs to an object — we'll cover functions properly in session 4.\n",)
Bash("change the case of a string:")
Bash("break a string into a list of substrings at a specified character. super useful for parsing comma-separated data (csv). if no character is given, it splits on spaces.")
Bash("find where a substring appears in a string. both return the position of the first match.")
Bash("> 🚧 **don't worry about mastering this yet!** this section is here to show you what's possible. come back to it once you're comfortable with the basics.\n",)
Bash("**regular expressions** (`regex` or `regexp`) are a powerful way to search for patterns in text. they're invaluable for:\n",)
Bash("- parsing log files and reports\n",)
Bash("you can experiment with regex interactively at [regex101](https://regex101.com/) or try the [free course on regexlearn](https://regexlearn.com/).\n",)
Bash("here's a practical example — extracting financial figures from apple's earnings report:")
Bash("- `,*\\d*` — optionally a comma followed by more digits\n",)
Bash("- `\\.*\\d*` — optionally a decimal point followed by more digits\n",)
Bash("regex looks cryptic at first, but it's just a compact way of describing patterns. you'll get there!")
Bash("and here's another pattern — finding words that follow \"apple\" in the text:")
